mission.title: Install and Configure Kyverno (Policy Engine)
mission.description: Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native policy engine that allows users to manage security and compliance policies as code. It is particularly useful for teams looking to enforce governance and automate policy management without needing to learn a new language. Key trade-offs include the need for a Kubernetes cluster and potential complexity in policy definitions.

steps.title: Add the Kyverno Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Kyverno Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration, allowing you to install Kyverno easily.

```bash

helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the Kyverno repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install Kyverno
steps.description:
This step installs the Kyverno policy engine in the 'kyverno' namespace using the specified version.

```bash

kubectl create namespace kyverno

helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version 1.17.1

```

> Creates a namespace for Kyverno.
> Installs Kyverno in the created namespace.

steps.title: Verify Kyverno installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the Kyverno pods are running correctly in the 'kyverno' namespace.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Lists all pods in the kyverno namespace to verify they are running.

steps.title: Create a sample policy
steps.description:
This step creates a sample Kyverno policy to demonstrate its functionality.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

```

> This command creates a policy that requires the 'app' label on Pods.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

steps.title: Verify the policy
steps.description:
This step checks if the policy is applied correctly by listing the policies in the cluster.

```bash

kubectl get cpol

```

> Lists all ClusterPolicies to verify the created policy.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Kyverno
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Kyverno installation and all associated resources. Be aware that any policies created will also be deleted.

```bash

helm uninstall kyverno --namespace kyverno

kubectl delete namespace kyverno

```

> Uninstalls Kyverno from the specified namespace.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Kyverno
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your policies. This step upgrades Kyverno to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get cpol -o yaml > kyverno-backup.yaml

helm upgrade kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version 1.17.1

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Backs up existing ClusterPolicies to a file.
> Upgrades Kyverno to the specified version.
> Verifies that the upgraded pods are running.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to install Kyverno: ...'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the Helm repository is not added correctly.

**Fix:**
Run 'helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno' and 'helm repo update'.

troubleshooting.title: Kyverno pod is in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state.
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This could be due to misconfiguration in the policies or insufficient resources.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs with 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kyverno' for details.

troubleshooting.title: Policy not enforcing as expected.
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The policy may not be matching the resources correctly.

**Fix:**
Review the policy definition and ensure it matches the intended resources.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'kubectl apply -f ...' fails with validation error.
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the policy is rejecting the resource due to validation rules.

**Fix:**
Check the policy rules and ensure the resource complies with them.

versionNotes.changes: Improved policy validation performance, added support for new Kubernetes API versions.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the Kyverno pods running with a status of 'Running' when executing 'kubectl get pods -n kyverno'.

        pattern:

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary tools installed.
